question: What does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) do according to the notes?

options.text: Answers from cached prior responses only, without retrieving new external context.
options.feedback: Caching past outputs is different from retrieval-augmented generation.

options.text: Retrieves relevant external documents at inference time and conditions generation without retraining model weights.
options.feedback: Correct: RAG grounds outputs using runtime context, not weight updates.

options.text: Fine-tunes the base model on retrieved passages before returning each user answer.
options.feedback: That describes continual training, while RAG keeps weights fixed at query time.

options.text: Only increases context-window length and does not fetch knowledge from external sources.
options.feedback: Longer context helps capacity, but RAG specifically requires retrieval.

whyQuestion: Why is RAG useful for personalized or current answers?

whyOptions.text: It permanently writes retrieved facts into model parameters after each request.
whyOptions.feedback: RAG context is transient; it does not mutate model weights.

whyOptions.text: It injects tenant-specific or recent evidence into prompts right before generation.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: retrieval supplies fresh, private context the base model lacks.

whyOptions.text: Vector similarity ranking guarantees correctness regardless of document quality.
whyOptions.feedback: Retrieval quality still controls grounding accuracy and hallucination risk.

whyOptions.text: It removes prompt-construction needs because token limits no longer apply.
whyOptions.feedback: Token budgets still matter even when retrieval is used.
